title: Output Format Control
concept: Forcing structured output (JSON, markdown tables, XML tags) via explicit schema instructions in the prompt.

exercises.prompt: Quick recap: Which instruction is more likely to get good results from a model?

options.text: Do not write a boring introduction.
Start with an engaging one-sentence hook.

exercises.explain: Telling the model what TO do ('start with an engaging hook') gives it a clear positive target, while negative instructions like 'don't be boring' leave it guessing what 'boring' even means to avoid.

cards.title: Same Answer, Wrong Shape
cards.body: Imagine asking a model for a list of ingredients and getting a beautiful paragraph back instead of a clean list. The information might be correct, but your app just crashed trying to read it. Format matters as much as content.

cards.title: What Is Output Format Control?
cards.body: It means telling the model EXACTLY how to structure its answer — like 'respond only in JSON with keys name, age, city' or 'use a markdown table with these columns.' You're specifying the shape of the output, not just its content.

cards.title: A Form Beats a Blank Page
cards.body: Asking someone to 'write about your trip' gets rambling paragraphs. Handing them a form with boxes labeled Destination, Dates, and Highlights gets clean, sortable answers every time. A schema in your prompt is that form.

cards.title: See It In Action
cards.body: Vague: 'List three fruits and their colors.' Structured: 'Return a JSON array where each item has "fruit" and "color" keys. Output ONLY the JSON, no extra text.' The second version gives you data your code can actually use directly.

cards.title: Myth: The Model Just 'Knows' the Format You Want
cards.body: Not true! Models default to natural, conversational prose unless told otherwise. If you need JSON, a table, or XML tags, you must explicitly say so — and showing a tiny example of the exact structure makes it far more reliable.

cards.title: Unstructured vs Structured Output
cards.body: Same request, two very different results depending on whether you specify a schema.

left.text: 'The capital of Japan is Tokyo, a bustling city with over 13 million people...' — hard to extract data from.

right.text: {"country": "Japan", "capital": "Tokyo", "population": 13000000} — ready to plug into your app instantly.

exercises.prompt: Which instruction is the BEST example of output format control?

options.text: Write a helpful and friendly response about the weather.
Don't be too technical in your answer.
Respond only with a markdown table with columns: City, Temperature, Condition.
Tell me about the weather in a few sentences.

exercises.explain: Option C explicitly defines the exact structure (a markdown table) and names the required columns, leaving no ambiguity about the output's shape.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If you don't specify an output format, the model will usually guess correctly and give you clean, parseable data every time.
exercises.explain: False — without explicit format instructions, models default to natural conversational text, which often includes extra explanations, varying structure, or missing fields that break automated parsing.

exercises.sentence: To make a model output data your app can parse, explicitly specify a schema like JSON in the prompt.
exercises.explain: A schema is a defined structure (like field names and format) that tells the model exactly how to organize its response, making it machine-readable.

exercises.prompt: Two developers wrote prompts asking for a list of book recommendations as structured data. Which response was generated by following STRICT format instructions (JSON with title/author keys, no extra text)?

options.text: Here are some great books! First, I'd recommend '1984' by George Orwell, a classic dystopian novel...

Sure! Book recommendations:
- 1984 (Orwell)
- Dune (Herbert)
Enjoy reading!

exercises.explain: Option B is clean JSON with only the requested keys and no conversational filler — exactly what strict format instructions with 'no extra text' would produce. The others include natural language wrapping that violates a strict schema request.

exercises.prompt: You're building an app that reads a model's output and displays it in a table on a webpage. Which prompt design choice matters MOST for making this reliable?

options.text: Ask nicely and hope the model formats it well
Explicitly specify the exact output schema (e.g., JSON with named fields) and show an example
Use only negative instructions like 'don't write paragraphs'
Make the prompt as short as possible

exercises.explain: Apps need predictable structure to parse and display data automatically. Explicitly stating the schema (field names, format, nesting) and showing a sample output removes guesswork, so the model's response reliably matches what your code expects — critical for anything beyond a human just reading text. Combined with what you've learned across this unit — clear roles, well-chosen examples, positive phrasing, and now strict format control — you're ready to write prompts that behave like real software components, not just chat messages.
